content:
# Exodus Chapter 15 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~41r-42r (PDF pp. 77-79); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 55-56 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_015_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- SHIRAH LAYOUT:** The Leningrad Codex writes the Song of the Sea (vv. 1b-18) in the distinctive "brick-over-half-brick" (*ariakh al gabei leveinah*) layout prescribed by the Talmud (Megillah 16b). Each line is arranged in three columns -- text, space, text -- with the blocks offset like bricks in a wall. This *shirah* layout is one of only two passages in the Torah given this treatment (the other being the Song of Moses, Deuteronomy 32). A *petuchah* marker appears before 15:1, opening a major literary unit. The *shirah* layout itself functions as the internal paragraph division within the Song. A *setumah* appears before v. 19 (prose resumption), another before v. 20 (Miriam's response), a *petuchah* before v. 22 (the wilderness transition), and a *setumah* before v. 27 (Elim).

---

## The Song of Moses -- Shirat ha-Yam (vv. 1-18)

**15:1** Then Moses and the sons of Israel sang this song to YHWH, and they spoke, saying: [fn1]

> "I will sing to YHWH, for triumphing, He has triumphed --
> the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea. [fn1b]

**15:2**

> YHWH is my strength and my song, Yah,
> and He has become my salvation.
> This is my God, and I will glorify Him,
> the God of my father, and I will exalt Him. [fn2]

**15:3**

> YHWH is a man of war;
> YHWH is His name. [fn3]

**15:4**

> The chariots of Pharaoh and his army
> He has cast into the sea,
> and the choice of his officers
> were drowned in the Sea of Reeds. [fn4]

**15:5**

> The deep waters covered them;
> they went down into the depths like a stone. [fn5]

---

**15:6**

> Your right hand, O YHWH, is majestic in power;
> Your right hand, O YHWH, shatters the enemy. [fn6]

**15:7**

> And in the greatness of Your majesty
> You overthrow those who rise against You.
> You send forth Your burning anger;
> it consumes them like stubble. [fn7]

**15:8**

> And by the blast of Your nostrils
> the waters were heaped up;
> the flowing waters stood firm like a heap;
> the deep waters congealed in the heart of the sea. [fn8]

**15:9**

> The enemy said,
> "I will pursue, I will overtake,
> I will divide the spoil;
> my appetite shall be filled with them.
> I will draw my sword;
> my hand shall dispossess them." [fn9]

**15:10**

> You blew with Your wind;
> the sea covered them.
> They sank like lead
> in the mighty waters. [fn10]

---

**15:11**

> Who is like You among the gods, O YHWH?
> Who is like You -- majestic in holiness,
> awesome in praises, doing wonders? [fn11]

**15:12**

> You stretched out Your right hand;
> the earth swallowed them. [fn12]

**15:13**

> You led in Your steadfast love
> the people whom You redeemed;
> You guided them in Your strength
> to Your holy dwelling. [fn13]

---

**15:14**

> The peoples heard; they trembled.
> Anguish seized the inhabitants of Philistia. [fn14]

**15:15**

> Then the chiefs of Edom were dismayed;
> the leaders of Moab -- trembling seized them.
> All the inhabitants of Canaan melted away. [fn15]

**15:16**

> Terror and dread fall upon them.
> By the greatness of Your arm
> they are still as a stone --
> until Your people pass over, O YHWH,
> until the people whom You acquired pass over. [fn16]

---

**15:17**

> You will bring them and plant them
> on the mountain of Your inheritance,
> the established place of Your dwelling which You have made, O YHWH,
> the sanctuary, O Lord, which Your hands have established. [fn17]

**15:18**

> YHWH shall reign
> forever and ever." [fn18]

---

## The Sea Returns over Egypt (v. 19)

**15:19** For the horse of Pharaoh, with his chariots and with his horsemen, went into the sea, and YHWH brought back the waters of the sea upon them. And the sons of Israel walked on the dry ground in the midst of the sea. [fn19]

---

## Miriam's Song (vv. 20-21)

**15:20** And Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took the tambourine in her hand, and all the women went out after her with tambourines and with dances. [fn20]

**15:21** And Miriam answered them: [fn21]

> "Sing to YHWH, for triumphing, He has triumphed --
> the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea."

---

## The Waters of Marah (vv. 22-25)

**15:22** And Moses caused Israel to journey from the Sea of Reeds, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur. And they went three days in the wilderness and did not find water. [fn22]

**15:23** And they came to Marah, and they were not able to drink the waters from Marah, for they were bitter. Therefore its name was called Marah. [fn23]

**15:24** And the people grumbled against Moses, saying, "What shall we drink?" [fn24]

**15:25** And he cried out to YHWH, and YHWH showed him a tree. And he cast it into the waters, and the waters became sweet. There He set for them a statute and an ordinance, and there He tested them. [fn25]

---

## YHWH the Healer (vv. 26-27)

**15:26** And He said, "If you will diligently listen to the voice of YHWH your God, and do what is right in His eyes, and give ear to His commandments, and keep all His statutes -- every disease which I put upon Egypt I will not put upon you, for I am YHWH your healer." [fn26]

**15:27** And they came to Elim, and there were twelve springs of water and seventy date palms. And they encamped there by the waters. [fn27]

---

> **[fn1] v.1a -- *Az yashir Mosheh uvnei Yisra'el*: "Then Moses and the sons of Israel sang" -- the prose introduction to the Song** -- Hebrew: *az yashir-Mosheh uvnei Yisra'el et-hashirah hazzot laYHWH vayyomru lemor*. The verb *yashir* (Qal imperfect of *shir*, "to sing") with *az* ("then") is an archaic Hebrew construction for past narration -- *az* + yiqtol functioning as a preterite (cf. Num. 21:17; Josh. 10:12; 1 Kgs. 8:12). The noun *hashirah hazzot* ("this song") designates the composition as a defined literary unit. EMET renders "Mosheh sang [yashir], and the sons of Yisra'el" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Moses and the sons of Israel sang this song to the LORD." PNEUMA renders "Moses and the Israelites sang this song to YHWH." The consensus follows LOGOS for formal structure ("Moses and the sons of Israel") and SOPHIA convention for YHWH transliterated. "Sons of Israel" is retained per project convention rather than PNEUMA's "Israelites." LXX: *tote ēsen Mōusēs kai hoi huioi Israēl tēn ōdēn tautēn tō theō* ("then Moses sang, and the sons of Israel, this song to God").

> **[fn1b] v.1b -- *Ki ga'oh ga'ah*: "triumphing, He has triumphed" -- the infinitive absolute refrain** -- Hebrew: *ashirah laYHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhvo ramah vayyam*. The cohortative *ashirah* ("I will sing! / Let me sing!") opens the Song with volitional force. The phrase *ki-ga'oh ga'ah* uses the infinitive absolute (*ga'oh*) followed by the perfect verb (*ga'ah*) from the root *g-'-h* ("to rise up, to be exalted, to surge"). This cognate accusative construction intensifies the verb absolutely: "rising up, He has risen up" / "triumphing, He has triumphed." The root *ga'ah* is used of the sea's surging (Job 38:11) and of divine majesty (Isa. 12:5), creating deliberate irony: the word for the sea's rising describes God's triumph over the sea. The verb *ramah* ("He has hurled") is a violent casting -- not a gentle dropping but a forceful throwing. EMET renders "he has risen in triumph [ga'oh ga'ah]." LOGOS renders "He is highly exalted [ga'oh ga'ah]." PNEUMA renders "he has triumphed gloriously." The consensus renders "triumphing, He has triumphed" -- preserving the infinitive absolute construction that is the signature rhetorical device of the refrain. This rendering captures both the intensification (the doubling) and the root sense of rising/triumph, while maintaining the verbal structure that English readers can feel as distinctive. LOGOS's "highly exalted" loses the verbal doubling; PNEUMA's "triumphed gloriously" converts the infinitive absolute into an adverb, losing the Hebrew's characteristic repetition. EMET's "risen in triumph" is close but uses a periphrastic phrase rather than preserving the doubling. LXX: *endoxōs gar dedoxastai* ("for He has been gloriously glorified") -- substituting *doxa* ("glory") for *ga'ah* ("exaltation/triumph"), a significant interpretive shift from rising to glorification.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Ozzi vezimrat Yah*: "my strength and my song, Yah" -- the *zimrat* crux** -- Hebrew: *ozzi vezimrat Yah vayehi-li lishu'ah zeh Eli ve'anvehu Elohei avi va'aromemenhu*. This verse contains one of the most debated phrases in Hebrew poetry. The noun *oz* ("strength, might") appears with a first-person suffix (*ozzi*, "my strength"). The word *zimrat* is either: (a) from the root *z-m-r* ("to sing, to make music"), yielding "my song" -- *zimrat Yah* = "the song of Yah"; or (b) from an alternative root *z-m-r* ("to be strong, to protect"), yielding "my protection/might" -- giving a hendiadys "my strength and my might is Yah." The LC reads the construct form *zimrat* (without yod suffix), connecting the noun directly to the abbreviated divine name *Yah* -- the short form of YHWH, appearing here for the first time in the Bible. This same phrase (*ozzi vezimrat Yah*) recurs in Isaiah 12:2 and Psalm 118:14. The demonstrative *zeh Eli* ("this is my God") is extraordinarily intimate -- a pointing word that transforms theology into personal encounter. The verb *ve'anvehu* is another crux: (a) from *navah* ("to beautify, to adorn"), yielding "I will beautify/glorify Him"; (b) from *naveh* ("dwelling"), yielding "I will make Him a dwelling" -- the rabbinic tradition reads both (b. Shabbat 133b). The Polel *va'aromemenhu* ("I will exalt Him," from *rum*, "to lift up") confirms the verse's trajectory of worship. EMET renders "my strength and my song -- Yah!" and "I will make Him a habitation" for *ve'anvehu*, following the *naveh* ("dwelling") derivation. LOGOS renders "my strength and my song, Yah" and "I will glorify Him," following the *navah* ("beautify") derivation. PNEUMA renders "my strength and my song" and "I will praise him." The consensus renders "my strength and my song, Yah" -- following LOGOS for "song" (the musical connotation is contextually appropriate within the Song), and "I will glorify Him" for *ve'anvehu* -- following LOGOS. "Glorify" captures the *navah* ("beautify, adorn") sense while aligning with the worship trajectory of the verse. EMET's "make Him a habitation" is the stronger etymological argument but creates a jarring semantic shift in mid-verse from praise to construction. The footnote preserves both readings. The LXX diverges significantly: *boēthos kai skepastēs* ("helper and protector") for *ozzi vezimrat Yah*, apparently reading *zimrat* as "protection" and suppressing the abbreviated name *Yah* entirely.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *YHWH ish milchamah*: "YHWH is a man of war" -- the divine warrior declaration** -- Hebrew: *YHWH ish milchamah YHWH shemo*. This is the most direct anthropomorphic divine warrior statement in the Hebrew Bible. The noun *ish* ("man") applied to YHWH is theologically bold -- God is metaphorically portrayed as a human warrior. The construct *ish milchamah* ("man of war, warrior") characterizes YHWH's nature as revealed in the Sea event. The second clause -- *YHWH shemo* ("YHWH is His name") -- grounds the warrior identity in the covenant name revealed at the burning bush (3:14-15). The juxtaposition of the anthropomorphic *ish milchamah* with the sacred name YHWH holds together the personal (warrior) and the transcendent (the Name). EMET renders "YHWH [is] a man of war [ish milkhamah]" -- literal with transliteration. LOGOS renders "The LORD is a man of war" -- literal with "the LORD." PNEUMA renders "YHWH is a warrior" -- dynamic, avoiding the anthropomorphism. The consensus renders "YHWH is a man of war" -- following EMET/LOGOS and preserving the anthropomorphic force of *ish milchamah*. PNEUMA's "warrior" is a valid English gloss for *ish milchamah* but eliminates the word *ish* ("man"), which is the theologically striking element. The Hebrew text does not soften the anthropomorphism, and neither should the translation. The LXX renders *suntribōn polemous* ("one who crushes wars"), deliberately avoiding the anthropomorphism and replacing the noun *ish* with a participial phrase -- a Hellenistic Jewish tendency to soften divine anthropomorphisms. The Hebrew makes no such concession.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- Pharaoh's chariots cast into the sea** -- Hebrew: *markevot Par'oh vecheilo yarah vayyam umivchar shalishav tubba'u veYam-Suf*. The verb *yarah* ("He has cast, hurled") is used for shooting arrows (1 Sam. 20:36) and casting lots (Josh. 18:6) -- YHWH is the archer; the chariots are His projectiles. The noun *mivchar* ("the choicest, the elite") from *bachar* ("to choose") specifies the elite officers, the *shalishim* ("officers," from *shalosh*, "three" -- originally the third man in a chariot crew). The passive *tubba'u* (Pual of *tava'*, "to sink") -- "they were drowned" -- emphasizes divine agency. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for rendering, with "Sea of Reeds" per convention for *Yam-Suf*. LXX renders *eruthrai thalassai* ("Red Sea") for Yam-Suf.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Tehomot*: "the deep waters" -- cosmic waters as instruments of judgment** -- Hebrew: *tehomot yekhasuymu yardu vimtsolot kemo-aven*. The noun *tehomot* (plural of *tehom*, "the deep, the primordial deep") evokes the *tehom* of Genesis 1:2 -- the chaotic waters before creation. The Sea event is a new act of creation: the same primordial waters that God mastered in Genesis now serve as instruments of judgment. The noun *metsolot* ("depths") intensifies the downward motion. The simile *kemo-aven* ("like a stone") is the first of three sinking similes: stone (v. 5), stubble consumed (v. 7), lead (v. 10). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *pontō ekalupsen autous* ("the sea covered them").

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Yeminekha YHWH ne'dari bakkoach*: "Your right hand, O YHWH, is majestic in power" -- anaphora and the right hand** -- Hebrew: *yeminekha YHWH ne'dari bakkoach yeminekha YHWH tir'ats oyev*. The repetition of *yeminekha YHWH* ("Your right hand, O YHWH") at the beginning of each line is anaphora -- deliberate repetition for rhetorical force. The Niphal participle *ne'dari* (from *adar*, "to be majestic, glorious") -- with the archaic *-i* ending characteristic of early Hebrew poetry -- describes the hand's quality. The verb *tir'ats* (Qal of *ra'ats*, "to shatter, crush") is violent: the right hand pulverizes. EMET renders "glorious [ne'dari] in power." LOGOS renders "majestic in power." PNEUMA renders "majestic in power." The consensus renders "majestic in power" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA. Both "glorious" (EMET) and "majestic" are valid for *ne'dari*; "majestic" is preferred because it preserves the verbal connection to *ne'dar* in v. 11 (*ne'dar baqqodesh*, "majestic in holiness"), creating the intended literary frame. LXX: *dedoxastai en ischui* ("has been glorified in strength").

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Ge'onekha* and *charonekha*: "Your majesty" and "Your burning anger"** -- Hebrew: *uverov ge'onekha taharos qamekha teshalach charonekha yokhlemo kaqqash*. The word *ge'on* ("majesty, exaltation") shares the root *ga'ah* with v. 1's *ga'oh ga'ah* -- the same "rising up" that described YHWH's triumph now describes His overwhelming majesty. The verb *taharos* (Qal of *haras*, "to tear down, demolish") is an architectural verb used for demolishing walls (Judg. 6:25). The irony: those who "rise against" God (*qamekha*, from *qum*, "to rise") are themselves torn down. The noun *charon* ("burning anger, fierce wrath") -- always used of divine anger, never human -- combined with *yokhlemo* ("consumes them," from *akhal*, "to eat") and *kaqqash* ("like stubble") creates an image of enemies consumed by divine fire. EMET renders "your burning wrath [kharonekha]." LOGOS renders "Your burning anger." PNEUMA renders "your burning anger." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Your burning anger" -- all three agree on this rendering. LXX: *ton thumon sou* ("Your wrath").

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ruach appekha* and *qafe'u tehomot*: "the blast of Your nostrils" and "the deep waters congealed" -- cosmic imagery** -- Hebrew: *uveruach appekha ne'ermu mayim nitsvu kemo-ned nozelim qafe'u tehomot belev-yam*. This verse is the cosmic center of the poem. The phrase *ruach appekha* ("wind/breath of Your nostrils") transforms the "east wind" of 14:21 into divine breath -- God's nostril-blast. The word *ruach* ("wind/breath/spirit") and *af* ("nostril/face/anger") combine: the same wind that physically drove the sea back is theologically the breathing of an angry God. The Niphal *ne'ermu* (from *aram*, "to heap up") describes the waters being piled. The noun *ned* ("heap, wall, dam") describes the piled waters as a wall. The verb *qafe'u* (Qal of *qafa'*, "to congeal, freeze, thicken, curdle") is extraordinary: the deep waters solidified -- liquid became solid. The *tehomot* froze in the *lev-yam* ("heart of the sea") -- the sea has a heart, and its heart stopped. EMET renders "the ruakh [wind/breath/spirit] of your nostrils [appekha]" and "the tehomot [deeps] congealed [qafe'u]" with extensive transliteration. LOGOS renders "the blast of Your nostrils" and "the deep waters congealed." PNEUMA renders "the blast of your nostrils" and "the deep waters congealed." All three agree on "blast of Your nostrils" and "congealed." The consensus follows LOGOS. The term *nozelim* ("flowing waters") -- participle of *nazal* ("to flow") -- denotes what should be flowing but has frozen in place; the consensus renders "flowing waters" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "surging waters stood firm like a wall" substitutes "wall" for *ned* ("heap"), harmonizing with 14:22 (*chomah*, "wall") -- the consensus preserves *ned* as "heap." LXX: *epagē hōsei teichos ta hudata* ("the waters were frozen like a wall"), interpreting *ned* ("heap") as *teichos* ("wall").

> **[fn9] v.9 -- The enemy's boast: six verbs of greed** -- Hebrew: *amar oyev erdof assig achaleq shalal timla'emo nafshi ariq charbi torishemo yadi*. Six first-person verbs in rapid-fire staccato capture the enemy's breathless greed: *erdof* ("I will pursue"), *assig* ("I will overtake"), *achaleq* ("I will divide"), *timla'emo nafshi* ("my appetite shall be filled"), *ariq charbi* ("I will draw my sword"), *torishemo yadi* ("my hand shall dispossess them"). No conjunctions, no pauses -- breathless panting of predatory desire. The word *nafshi* here means "appetite/throat" rather than "soul" -- the hunger is visceral. The verb *ariq* (Hiphil of *ruq*, "to empty") -- "emptying" the sword from its scabbard -- also hints that the sword will accomplish nothing. EMET renders "my nefesh [soul/appetite] will be filled" and "my hand will dispossess them." LOGOS renders "my soul shall be filled" and "my hand shall dispossess them." PNEUMA renders "my appetite will gorge on them" and "my hand will destroy them." The consensus renders "my appetite shall be filled with them" -- following PNEUMA's identification of *nafshi* as "appetite" (contextually more precise than "soul") but retaining the formal verbal structure of LOGOS ("shall be filled" rather than PNEUMA's "will gorge"). For *torishemo*, the consensus renders "shall dispossess them" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The Hiphil of *yarash* means "to dispossess, to drive out," not "to destroy" (PNEUMA). LXX: *emplēsō psuchēn mou* ("I will fill my soul").

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Nashafta beruchakha*: "You blew with Your wind" -- one divine breath against six human verbs** -- Hebrew: *nashafta beruchakha kissamo yam tsalalu ka'oferet bemayim addirim*. Against the enemy's six verbs in v. 9, God's response is one verb: *nashafta* (Qal of *nashaf*, "to blow" -- a rare verb, occurring only here and Isa. 40:24). One divine breath undoes six acts of human aggression. The simile *ka'oferet* ("like lead") is the second sinking image -- heavier than stone (v. 5), lead sinks faster and more decisively. The *mayim addirim* ("mighty waters") uses *addir* ("mighty, majestic, noble") -- the same root as *ne'dari* in v. 6. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *edisan hōsei molibos en hudati sphodrō* ("they sank like lead in violent water").

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Mi kamokha ba'elim YHWH*: "Who is like You among the gods, O YHWH?" -- the theological apex** -- Hebrew: *mi-kamokha ba'elim YHWH mi kamokha ne'dar baqqodesh nora tehillot oseh fele*. This is the theological climax of the Song and one of the most important verses in the Hebrew Bible for understanding Israelite theology. The phrase *ba'elim* ("among the gods") uses the plural of *el* ("god, divine being"), acknowledging other divine beings while asserting YHWH's absolute supremacy. This is not philosophical monotheism ("no other gods exist") but monolatrous exaltation: whatever other *elim* exist, none compares to YHWH. The question *mi kamokha* became one of the most important liturgical phrases in Judaism (recited daily in Shacharit) and gives rise to the name Maccabee (traditionally an acronym of *Mi Kamokha Ba'elim YHWH*). Three predicates follow: *ne'dar baqqodesh* ("majestic in holiness"), *nora tehillot* ("awesome in praises" / "fearful in praiseworthy deeds"), and *oseh fele* ("doing wonders" -- singular *fele*, emphasizing the singular, unrepeatable wonder). The Niphal participle *ne'dar* (from *adar*) echoes *ne'dari* in v. 6, creating a literary frame: majestic in power (v. 6), majestic in holiness (v. 11). EMET renders "who is like you [mi kamokha] among the elim [gods]" and "fearsome [nora] in praises [tehillot]." LOGOS renders "Who is like You among the gods" and "fearful in praises." PNEUMA renders "Who is like you among the gods" and "awesome in glory, working wonders." The consensus renders "Who is like You among the gods, O YHWH?" -- following LOGOS, with YHWH transliterated per convention. For *nora tehillot*, the consensus renders "awesome in praises" -- "awesome" (following PNEUMA's register) captures the Niphal of *yare'* ("to fear") in modern English more effectively than "fearful" (LOGOS) or "fearsome" (EMET), which could imply God is frightened rather than awe-inspiring. "In praises" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained over PNEUMA's "in glory" because *tehillot* means "praises/songs of praise," not "glory" (*kavod*). For *oseh fele*, the consensus renders "doing wonders" (LOGOS/EMET) rather than PNEUMA's "working wonders" -- both are valid but "doing" preserves the common Qal participle *oseh* more precisely. LXX: *tis homoios soi en theois kurie* ("who is like You among the gods, O Lord") -- preserving the divine council language. The LXX renders *baqqodesh* as *en hagiois* ("among the holy ones"), shifting from abstract holiness to holy beings (angels).

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Tivla'emo arets*: "the earth swallowed them"** -- Hebrew: *natita yeminekha tivla'emo arets*. The verb *tivla'emo* (Qal of *bala'*, "to swallow, engulf") with *arets* ("earth/land") as subject creates a tension: the Egyptians drowned in the sea, not the earth. The term *arets* here likely functions as "the underworld" (Sheol, the land of the dead) or represents a merism in which both sea and earth participate in destruction. The verb *bala'* will become the defining verb of Korah's rebellion (Num. 16:32). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *katepien autous gē* ("the earth swallowed them").

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Vechasdekha* and *am-zu ga'alta*: "in Your steadfast love" and "the people whom You redeemed" -- *chesed* and kinsman-redemption** -- Hebrew: *nachita vechasdekha am-zu ga'alta nehalta be'azzekha el-neveh qodshekha*. The verb *nachita* (Qal of *nachah*, "to lead, guide") describes God's role as shepherd-guide. The noun *chesed* (here *vechasdekha*, "in/by Your *chesed*") is one of the most theologically significant words in the Hebrew Bible -- denoting steadfast love, covenant loyalty, faithful kindness. It combines commitment (covenant faithfulness), affection (love), and reliability (steadfastness). The demonstrative *zu* is archaic, found primarily in early poetry (cf. Ps. 68:9; Isa. 42:24). The verb *ga'al* ("to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer") implies family obligation -- the *go'el* is the family member who buys back property, avenges blood, or restores a relative's fortunes. God's redemption of Israel is framed in kinship terms. The Piel *nehalta* (from *nahal*, "to lead to water, to guide") specifically describes leading flocks to watering places. The *neveh qodshekha* ("Your holy dwelling") -- from *neveh* ("pasture, abode, dwelling") -- could refer to Sinai, Canaan, Jerusalem, or the heavenly dwelling; the ambiguity is deliberately expansive. EMET renders "your khesed [lovingkindness/steadfast love]" and "the habitation [neveh] of your qodesh [holiness]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Your steadfast love" and "Your holy dwelling." PNEUMA renders "your steadfast love" and "your holy dwelling place." The consensus renders "Your steadfast love" (following LOGOS/PNEUMA -- all three agree *chesed* should convey covenantal loyalty; "steadfast love" has become the standard English rendering in formal translations) and "Your holy dwelling" (following LOGOS -- "dwelling" preserves *neveh* without the addition of "place" [PNEUMA]). The LXX notably renders *vechasdekha* as *tē dikaiosunē sou* ("by Your righteousness"), substituting "righteousness" for "steadfast love" -- a significant theological shift that interprets the Exodus as an act of divine justice rather than covenant loyalty.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- The nations tremble** -- Hebrew: *shame'u ammim yirgazun chil achaz yoshvei Peleshet*. The verb *yirgazun* (Qal of *ragaz*, "to tremble, quake") with the archaic *-un* ending is characteristic of early Hebrew poetry. The noun *chil* ("anguish, writhing, labor-pain distress" -- from *chul*, "to writhe") evokes the twisting agony of a woman in labor. The geographical sweep (Philistia, Edom, Moab, Canaan, vv. 14-16) traces Israel's coming route from Egypt to the promised land. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *ōdines elabon katoikountas Phulistiim* ("birth-pangs seized the inhabitants of Philistia").

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Allufei Edom* and *eilei Mo'av*: "chiefs of Edom" and "leaders of Moab"** -- Hebrew: *az nivhalu allufei Edom eilei Mo'av yochaazemo ra'ad namogu kol yoshvei Kena'an*. The noun *alluf* ("chief, chieftain, clan-leader") is from *elef* ("thousand, clan"). The noun *ayil* ("ram, mighty one, leader") derives from the image of the lead ram of a flock. The Niphal *nivhalu* (from *bahal*, "to be terrified") describes panic. The Niphal *namogu* (from *mug*, "to melt, dissolve") anticipates Rahab's confession in Joshua 2:9-11. EMET renders "the mighty ones [eilei] of Mo'av" -- etymologically precise. LOGOS renders "the leaders of Moab." PNEUMA renders "the leaders of Moab." The consensus renders "the leaders of Moab" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) -- "leaders" captures the functional meaning of *eilei* as political/military figures without requiring the reader to decode "mighty ones." For *nivhalu*, the consensus renders "were dismayed" (LOGOS) rather than "were terrified" (EMET/PNEUMA) -- the Niphal of *bahal* covers a range from dismay to terror; "dismayed" preserves the formal register while conveying the paralysis of leadership. LXX: *espeeusan hēgemones Edōm* ("the rulers of Edom hastened/trembled").

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Yiddemu ka'aven* and *am-zu qanita*: "still as a stone" and "the people whom You acquired"** -- Hebrew: *tippol aleihem ematah vafachad bigdol zero'akha yiddemu ka'aven ad-ya'avor ammekha YHWH ad-ya'avor am-zu qanita*. The paired terror-words (*ematah*, "terror" + *fachad*, "dread") intensify the paralysis. The verb *yiddemu* (Qal of *damam*, "to be still, silent, motionless") means the nations become petrified -- unable to move or speak. The stone simile (*ka'aven*) recalls v. 5, but here the enemies are frozen in fear rather than sinking in water. The repeated *ad-ya'avor* ("until they pass over") anticipates the crossing of the Jordan. The verb *qanita* (Qal of *qanah*, "to acquire, buy, create") -- *am-zu qanita* ("the people whom You acquired") -- can mean both "acquire" and "create" (cf. Gen. 14:19), suggesting Israel's redemption is simultaneously a purchase and a new creation. EMET renders "they are still [yidemu] like a stone" and "which you acquired [qanita]." LOGOS renders "they are still as a stone" and "You have acquired." PNEUMA renders "they were still as stone" and "the people you acquired." All three agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, rendering "still as a stone" and "the people whom You acquired." LXX: *apolithōthētōsan* ("let them be turned to stone") -- a striking rendering that makes the stone-simile into literal petrification.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Behar nachalatekha* and *miqdash*: "the mountain of Your inheritance" and "the sanctuary" -- temple theology** -- Hebrew: *tevi'emo vitta'emo behar nachalatekha makhon leshivtekha pa'alta YHWH miqdash Adonai konenu yadekha*. The verbs *tevi'emo* (Hiphil of *bo'*, "to bring") and *vitta'emo* (Qal of *nata'*, "to plant") form a pair: God brings and plants. The agricultural metaphor of "planting" a people is deeply rooted in the Hebrew Bible (2 Sam. 7:10; Jer. 24:6; Amos 9:15). The destination is described in four parallel terms: *har nachalatekha* ("the mountain of Your inheritance"), *makhon leshivtekha* ("the established place for Your dwelling"), *miqdash* ("the sanctuary"), and *konenu yadekha* ("Your hands have established"). Whether this verse envisions Sinai, the Jerusalem temple mount, or an idealized divine dwelling is debated -- the Song's language is deliberately expansive. The noun *makhon* ("established place, fixed place, foundation") is used of the heavenly dwelling (1 Kgs. 8:39, 43, 49). The Polel *konenu* (from *kun*, "to be firm") with *yadekha* ("Your hands" -- dual, both hands) attributes the sanctuary's construction to God's own hands. EMET renders "the mountain [be-har] of your inheritance [nakhalatekha]" and "the miqdash [sanctuary], O Adonai" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the mountain of Your inheritance" and "the sanctuary, O Lord." PNEUMA renders "the mountain that is your own" and "the sanctuary, Lord, that your hands established." The consensus renders "the mountain of Your inheritance" (EMET/LOGOS -- the literal rendering preserving the theological weight of *nachalah* as both "inheritance" and "possession") and "the sanctuary, O Lord" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "the mountain that is your own" loses the specific noun *nachalah* ("inheritance"), which carries connotations of permanent, divinely allocated property. For *makhon leshivtekha*, the consensus renders "the established place of Your dwelling which You have made" -- combining LOGOS's "place of Your dwelling" with the relative clause *pa'alta* ("which You have made"), which PNEUMA's "the place you have made for your dwelling" also captures. LXX: *ep' orous klēronomias sou* ("upon the mountain of Your inheritance") and *hagiasma* ("sanctuary") for *miqdash*.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *YHWH yimlokh le'olam va'ed*: "YHWH shall reign forever and ever" -- the climactic declaration** -- Hebrew: *YHWH yimlokh le'olam va'ed*. This is the theological destination of the entire Song. The verb *yimlokh* (Qal imperfect of *malakh*, "to reign") declares God's kingship -- the Sea event has demonstrated not merely that God is powerful but that He is king. The phrase *le'olam va'ed* ("forever and ever," literally "to eternity and further") is the strongest temporal expression available in Hebrew. The Song's trajectory moves from specific event (vv. 1-5) through warrior-power (vv. 6-10) through incomparability (vv. 11-13) through eschatological anticipation (vv. 14-17) to universal conclusion: God reigns forever. This verse is recited daily in Jewish liturgy and is central to the theology of divine kingship (*malkhut*). EMET renders "YHWH will reign [yimlokh] le-olam va'ed [forever and ever]!" LOGOS renders "The LORD will reign forever and ever." PNEUMA renders "YHWH will reign forever and ever!" The consensus renders "YHWH shall reign forever and ever" -- using "shall" rather than "will" (EMET/LOGOS/PNEUMA) to convey the declarative, prophetic force of the imperfect *yimlokh* in this climactic context. "Shall" in English carries a note of inevitability and decree beyond simple futurity. LXX: *kurios basileuōn ton aiōna kai ep' aiōna kai eti* ("the Lord reigning for the age and upon the age and still").

> **[fn19] v.19 -- The prose colophon** -- Hebrew: *ki va sus Par'oh berikhbo uvfarashav bayyam vayyashev YHWH aleihem et-mei hayyam uvnei Yisra'el halekhu vayyabbashah betokh hayyam*. This prose verse recapitulates the core event: Pharaoh's forces entered the sea; YHWH returned (*vayyashev*, Hiphil of *shuv*) the waters; Israel walked on dry ground. The singular *sus* ("horse") is collective. The verb *vayyashev* ("brought back") is the same root used for Israel's "turning back" in 14:2 -- the waters return just as Israel was commanded to return. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, with "sons of Israel" per convention. LXX: *epēgagen ep' autous kurios to hudōr tēs thalassēs* ("the Lord brought back the water of the sea upon them").

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Miriam hannevi'ah achot Aharon*: "Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron" -- the first prophetess** -- Hebrew: *vattiqqach Miriam hannevi'ah achot Aharon et-hatof beyadah vattetse'na khol-hannashim achareha betuppim uvimcholot*. Miriam is given the title *hannevi'ah* ("the prophetess"), making her the first woman in the Torah to receive this designation. The title *navi'/nevi'ah* ("prophet/prophetess") denotes one who speaks on behalf of God. She is identified as *achot Aharon* ("the sister of Aaron") rather than "the sister of Moses" -- possibly reflecting an older tradition, or because Moses is already prominent as Song-leader. The *tof* ("tambourine, hand drum") is a frame drum beaten by hand, associated with women's victory celebrations (Judg. 11:34; 1 Sam. 18:6). The *mecholot* ("dances") from *chul/chol* ("to whirl, to dance") describe ecstatic circle-dancing. EMET renders "Miriam the neviah [prophetess], the sister of Aharon" with transliteration and "tof [tambourine/hand-drum]." LOGOS renders "Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron" and "timbrel." PNEUMA renders "Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister" and "a tambourine." The consensus renders "Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron" (following LOGOS for formal structure) and "the tambourine" (preserving the Hebrew definite article *ha-tof*). "Tambourine" is preferred over LOGOS's "timbrel" (archaic) and EMET's transliterated "tof." PNEUMA's "Aaron's sister" is the possessive form, but the Hebrew construct *achot Aharon* ("sister of Aaron") is better preserved as "the sister of Aaron" for formal register. LXX: *hē prophētis hē adelphē Aarōn* ("the prophetess, the sister of Aaron").

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Vatta'an lahem Miriam*: "And Miriam answered them" -- antiphonal worship** -- Hebrew: *vatta'an lahem Miriam shiru laYHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhvo ramah vayyam*. The verb *vatta'an* (Qal of *anah*, "to answer, to respond, to sing responsively") indicates antiphonal performance: Miriam "answers" or "responds to" the men's Song by leading the women in the refrain. The pronoun *lahem* ("to them") is masculine plural, suggesting Miriam responds to Moses and the men of Israel. The shift from *ashirah* ("I will sing," 1cs, v. 1) to *shiru* ("Sing!" 2mp imperative) transforms the Song from personal praise to communal exhortation. Many scholars consider Miriam's two-line couplet to be the oldest form of the victory tradition -- the original victory cry later expanded into the full Song. EMET renders "Miriam answered [vatta'an] to them." LOGOS renders "Miriam answered them." PNEUMA renders "Miriam sang to them." The consensus renders "Miriam answered them" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The verb *anah* in musical contexts means "to respond antiphonally," not merely "to sing" (PNEUMA). Preserving "answered" maintains the call-and-response structure essential to the verse's liturgical significance. LXX: *exērchen gar autois Mariam* ("for Miriam led them"), interpreting *anah* as "to lead [a song]."

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Vayyassa Mosheh*: "Moses caused Israel to journey" -- the wilderness transition** -- Hebrew: *vayyassa Mosheh et-Yisra'el miYam-Suf vayyetse'u el-midbar-Shur vayyelekkhu sheloshet-yamim bammidbar velo-matse'u mayim*. The Hiphil *vayyassa* (from *nasa'*, "to pull up stakes, journey") makes Moses the active agent. The *midbar-Shur* ("wilderness of Shur") -- *Shur* possibly meaning "wall," referring to the Egyptian border fortification system -- is the transition zone between the sea and the interior desert. The three-day period (*sheloshet yamim*) echoes Moses' original request to Pharaoh (3:18; 5:3; 8:27). The narrative shift from ecstatic victory to desperate thirst is abrupt and theologically deliberate. EMET renders "Mosheh caused Yisra'el to journey [vayassa]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Moses caused Israel to journey." PNEUMA renders "Moses led Israel away from the Sea of Reeds." The consensus renders "Moses caused Israel to journey" (following EMET/LOGOS) -- preserving the Hiphil causative force of *vayyassa*. PNEUMA's "led Israel away" loses the specific verb (*nasa'*, "to pull up stakes") and its connotation of organized camp movement. LXX: *exēren Mōusēs tous huious Israēl* ("Moses lifted up the sons of Israel").

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Marah* and *marim*: the wordplay of bitterness** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo'u Maratah velo yakhlu lishtot mayim miMarah ki marim hem al-ken qara-shemah Marah*. The place-name *Marah* (from *marar*, "to be bitter") creates a concentrated etymological wordplay: *Marah* (place-name) + *marim* (description: "bitter") + *Marah* (repeated). The bitterness is literal (undrinkable water) and symbolic (the bitter testing of the wilderness). The suffix *-ah* (he-locale) on *Maratah* indicates direction: "toward Marah." All three translators preserve the wordplay. EMET renders "Marah... marim... Marah" with full transliteration. LOGOS renders "Marah... bitter... Marah." PNEUMA renders "Marah... bitter... Marah," adding the explanatory "that is why the place is called Marah." The consensus follows LOGOS: "Marah... bitter... Marah" -- preserving the Hebrew name untranslated so the English reader can hear the echo between the place-name and the adjective "bitter." The LXX translates the place-name: *Pikria* ("Bitterness").

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Vayyillonu*: "the people grumbled" -- the inaugural complaint** -- Hebrew: *vayyillonu ha'am al-Mosheh lemor mah-nishteh*. The verb *vayyillonu* (from *lun*, "to grumble, murmur, complain") introduces the wilderness complaint motif that will dominate the next forty years. This is the first use of this verb in the wilderness narrative. The question *mah-nishteh* ("What shall we drink?") is reasonable on its face but theologically devastating: three days after the sea split, they cannot trust God for drinking water. EMET renders "the people grumbled [vayillonu]." LOGOS renders "the people murmured." PNEUMA renders "the people complained." The consensus renders "the people grumbled" -- following EMET. "Grumbled" is preferred over "murmured" (LOGOS, slightly archaic) and "complained" (PNEUMA, too modern/neutral for the loaded Hebrew term *lun*, which becomes a technical term for covenantal rebellion in the wilderness). LXX: *diegogguzen ho laos* ("the people murmured").

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Vayyorehu YHWH ets*: "YHWH showed him a tree" -- the Torah-root of healing** -- Hebrew: *vayyits'aq el-YHWH vayyorehu YHWH ets vayyashleikh el-hammayim vayyimtequ hammayim sham sam lo choq umishpat vesham nissahu*. The verb *vayyorehu* (Hiphil of *yarah*, "to teach, instruct, show, point out") is the same root that gives the noun *Torah* ("instruction, teaching, law"). YHWH "torah-ed" Moses -- He showed/instructed him. The object is *ets* ("tree, wood") -- the text does not specify the species. The transformation of bitter to sweet (*vayyimtequ*, from *mataq*, "to be sweet") through a divinely indicated tree becomes a prophetic pattern: the curse is reversed by an unlikely instrument. The enigmatic clause *sham sam lo choq umishpat* ("there He set for them a statute and an ordinance") introduces legislation whose specific content is not identified. The verb *nissahu* (Piel of *nasah*, "to test, try, prove") makes clear that the bitter water was a deliberate divine test. EMET renders "YHWH showed him [vayorehu] a tree [ets]" and "a khoq [statute] and a mishpat [judgment]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the LORD showed him a tree" and "a statute and an ordinance." PNEUMA renders "YHWH showed him a piece of wood" and "a statute and a rule." The consensus renders "YHWH showed him a tree" (following EMET/LOGOS -- "tree" preserves the Hebrew *ets*, which denotes a living tree; PNEUMA's "piece of wood" implies already-hewn timber, which the Hebrew does not specify) and "a statute and an ordinance" (following LOGOS -- *choq umishpat* as a legal word-pair is best rendered with the traditional "statute and ordinance"; PNEUMA's "rule" is too informal for *mishpat*). LXX: *edeixen autō kurios xulon* ("the Lord showed him a tree/piece of wood").

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ki ani YHWH rofekha*: "I am YHWH your healer" -- the divine physician** -- Hebrew: *im-shamo'a tishma leqol YHWH Elohekha vehayashar be'einav ta'aseh veha'azanta lemitsvotav veshamarta kol-chuqqav kol-hammachalah asher-samti beMitsrayim lo-asim alekha ki ani YHWH rofekha*. The construction *shamo'a tishma* (infinitive absolute + imperfect of *shama'*, "to hear, listen, obey") is the cognate accusative for emphatic intensification: "hearing, you shall hear" / "you shall diligently listen." The conditional structure (*im*, "if") establishes the covenant pattern: obedience yields divine protection. The *machalah* ("disease, sickness") placed upon Egypt refers to the plagues, now reframed as diseases. The climactic self-revelation *ki ani YHWH rofekha* ("for I am YHWH your healer") introduces a new divine title: YHWH Rofe' -- YHWH the Healer. The participle *rofekha* (Qal active participle of *rafa'*, "to heal, cure, restore" + 2ms suffix) is a divine self-designation. The root *rafa'* encompasses physical healing (Gen. 20:17), national restoration (2 Chr. 7:14), and spiritual renewal (Ps. 103:3). The God who inflicted plagues also heals -- the same God who made the waters bitter makes them sweet. EMET renders "If you will diligently listen [shamo'a tishma]" and "I [am] YHWH your healer [rofekha]." LOGOS renders "If you will diligently listen" and "I am the LORD your healer." PNEUMA renders "If you will truly listen" and "I am YHWH, your healer." All three agree on substance. The consensus renders "If you will diligently listen" (following EMET/LOGOS -- "diligently" captures the infinitive absolute intensification better than PNEUMA's "truly") and "I am YHWH your healer" (combining LOGOS's formal structure with SOPHIA convention for YHWH transliterated; the comma in PNEUMA's "YHWH, your healer" is omitted to reflect the Hebrew's construct-like directness: *YHWH rofekha*). LXX: *egō eimi kurios ho iōmenos se* ("I am the Lord the one healing you").

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Elim*: twelve springs and seventy palms -- the oasis of grace** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo'u Elimah vesham shtem esreh enot mayim veshiv'im temarim vayyachanu-sham al-hammayim*. The name *Elim* (possibly from *elim*, "great trees, terebinths" or related to *el*, "mighty one") denotes a place of abundance. The numbers are symbolically loaded: twelve springs (*shtem esreh enot mayim*) for the twelve tribes; seventy palm trees (*shiv'im temarim*) corresponding to the seventy elders (Num. 11:16) or the seventy descendants of Jacob (Gen. 46:27). After the bitter waters of Marah, Elim is the oasis of grace -- divine provision following divine testing. The chapter ends not with sermon but with campsite, the simplest image of divine care. All three translators agree on substance. EMET renders "palm trees [temarim]." LOGOS renders "date palms." PNEUMA renders "palm trees." The consensus renders "date palms" -- following LOGOS. The Hebrew *tamar* specifically denotes the date palm (*Phoenix dactylifera*), the iconic tree of desert oases, rather than a generic palm. LXX: *stelechē phoinikōn* ("trunks of palms").

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. *Ga'oh ga'ah* (vv. 1, 21): "triumphing, He has triumphed"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he has risen in triumph" | Literal: root *ga'ah* as "rise up" |
| LOGOS | "He is highly exalted" | Formal: adverbial intensification |
| PNEUMA | "he has triumphed gloriously" | Dynamic: adverbial gloss |

The infinitive absolute construction *ga'oh ga'ah* is the Song's signature phrase, repeated in Miriam's refrain (v. 21). The consensus renders "triumphing, He has triumphed" -- preserving the doubling structure of the Hebrew cognate accusative (infinitive absolute + perfect verb) in a way that English readers can feel as distinctive. LOGOS's "highly exalted" collapses the doubling into an adverb-adjective pair. PNEUMA's "triumphed gloriously" also uses an adverb but substitutes "gloriously" (which belongs to the *kavod*/*doxa* semantic field) for the *ga'ah* root ("to rise up, surge, triumph"). EMET's "risen in triumph" captures the root meaning well but uses a periphrastic phrase rather than the verbal doubling. The consensus's "triumphing, He has triumphed" mirrors the Hebrew's X-ing, He has X-ed pattern, preserving both the root and the rhetorical force.

### 2. *Ozzi vezimrat Yah* (v. 2): "my strength and my song, Yah"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "my strength and my song -- Yah!" | Literal: exclamatory Yah |
| LOGOS | "my strength and my song, Yah" | Formal: Yah as appositive |
| PNEUMA | "my strength and my song" | Dynamic: Yah absorbed |

All three render *zimrat* as "song" rather than the alternative "protection/strength." The consensus follows this majority, noting the musical context. The abbreviated divine name *Yah* is retained in the consensus (EMET/LOGOS) rather than absorbed (PNEUMA), since it is the first occurrence of this name-form in the Bible and carries independent theological significance.

### 3. *Ve'anvehu* (v. 2): "I will glorify Him"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will make Him a habitation" | Literal: from *naveh* ("dwelling") |
| LOGOS | "I will glorify Him" | Formal: from *navah* ("beautify") |
| PNEUMA | "I will praise him" | Dynamic: worship-oriented gloss |

This is a genuine crux. EMET follows the *naveh* ("dwelling") derivation, which the Targumim and many rabbis support ("I will build Him a sanctuary"). LOGOS follows the *navah* ("beautify, adorn") derivation, rendering "glorify." PNEUMA's "praise" is a general worship term that avoids the crux. The consensus follows LOGOS: "I will glorify Him." The parallel verb *va'aromemenhu* ("I will exalt Him") confirms the verse's trajectory of worship and praise, making "glorify" the more contextually coherent choice. The dwelling interpretation, though important in rabbinic tradition, creates a jarring semantic shift from praise to construction within the same poetic line.

### 4. *Ish milchamah* (v. 3): "a man of war"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a man of war [ish milkhamah]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "a man of war" | Formal: preserves anthropomorphism |
| PNEUMA | "a warrior" | Dynamic: avoids anthropomorphism |

The consensus retains "a man of war" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "a warrior" (PNEUMA). The word *ish* ("man") applied to YHWH is the theologically striking element -- it is an anthropomorphism so bold that the LXX avoids it (*suntribōn polemous*, "one who crushes wars"). PNEUMA's "warrior" is a valid gloss for *ish milchamah* as a phrase, but it eliminates the word "man" that gives this verse its theological force. The Hebrew text does not soften the anthropomorphism, and the translation should preserve it.

### 5. *Chesed* (v. 13): "steadfast love"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "khesed [lovingkindness/steadfast love]" | Literal: transliterated with glosses |
| LOGOS | "steadfast love" | Formal: standard translation |
| PNEUMA | "steadfast love" | Dynamic: same as formal |

Unanimous agreement on "steadfast love" as the rendering for *chesed*. This is one of the rare cases where all three translators converge. "Steadfast love" has become the standard English rendering in formal translations (ESV, NRSV), capturing the covenantal loyalty dimension better than the older "lovingkindness" (KJV/ASV) or the generic "mercy" (earlier translations). The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

### 6. *Mi kamokha ba'elim* (v. 11): "among the gods"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "among the elim [gods]" | Literal: transliterated with gloss |
| LOGOS | "among the gods" | Formal: direct rendering |
| PNEUMA | "among the gods" | Dynamic: direct rendering |

Unanimous agreement on preserving the divine council language. All three render *ba'elim* as "among the gods," acknowledging the monolatrous framework of the Song. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering without softening (e.g., "among the mighty" or "among the heavenly beings" -- alternative renderings that obscure the theological force of the passage).

### 7. *Nafshi* (v. 9): "my appetite"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "my nefesh [soul/appetite]" | Literal: transliterated with both glosses |
| LOGOS | "my soul" | Formal: standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "my appetite" | Dynamic: contextual rendering |

The consensus renders "my appetite" -- following PNEUMA. In this specific context -- the enemy's breathless greed for plunder -- *nafshi* functions as "appetite/throat" (the concrete sense of *nefesh*) rather than "soul" (the abstract sense). The enemy's hunger for spoil is visceral, located in the gut, not the intellect. LOGOS's "soul" is the standard default rendering but misses the physical, predatory connotation in this verse.

### 8. *Har nachalatekha* (v. 17): "the mountain of Your inheritance"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the mountain of your inheritance [nakhalatekha]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "the mountain of Your inheritance" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the mountain that is your own" | Dynamic: paraphrase |

EMET and LOGOS agree on the literal rendering. PNEUMA's "the mountain that is your own" loses the specific noun *nachalah* ("inheritance, hereditary possession"), which carries connotations of permanent, divinely allocated property -- a term with deep theological resonance in the land-promise tradition. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

### 9. *Rofekha* (v. 26): "your healer"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your healer [rofekha]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "your healer" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "your healer" | Dynamic |

Unanimous agreement. All three render the Qal active participle *rofekha* as "your healer." The consensus follows the unanimous rendering. This divine self-designation -- YHWH Rofe' -- introduces the healer dimension of God's character, complementing the warrior title (*ish milchamah*, v. 3). The God who destroys also heals.

### 10. *Hannevi'ah* (v. 20): "the prophetess"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the neviah [prophetess]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "the prophetess" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the prophetess" | Dynamic |

Unanimous agreement on "the prophetess" for *hannevi'ah*. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering. Miriam is the first woman in the Torah to receive this title.

### 11. Marah Wordplay (v. 23): preserving the bitter echo

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Marah... marim... Marah" | Literal: full transliteration |
| LOGOS | "Marah... bitter... Marah" | Formal: name + translation |
| PNEUMA | "Marah... bitter... Marah" | Dynamic: with explanatory addition |

All three preserve the wordplay by leaving *Marah* untranslated as a proper name while rendering *marim* as "bitter," allowing the English reader to hear the echo between the name and the adjective. The consensus follows LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "that is why the place is called Marah" -- an explanatory expansion that, while helpful, is not in the Hebrew (which reads *al-ken qara shemah Marah*, "therefore its name was called Marah"). The consensus preserves the Hebrew's own formulation.

### 12. Poetic Line Breaks and Stanza Structure

The Song of the Sea (vv. 1b-18) is rendered with poetic line breaks preserving the parallelism and stanza structure visible in the Leningrad Codex's *shirah* layout. The five stanzas follow the theological progression identified by all three translators:

- **Stanza I (vv. 1b-5):** The specific event -- the drowning of Egypt's army
- **Stanza II (vv. 6-10):** YHWH's warrior-power -- the right hand and the divine breath
- **Stanza III (vv. 11-13):** The incomparability of YHWH -- *mi kamokha* and *chesed*
- **Stanza IV (vv. 14-16):** The terror of the nations -- Philistia, Edom, Moab, Canaan
- **Stanza V (vv. 17-18):** The sanctuary and the eternal reign

Section breaks (horizontal rules) in the consensus text mark these stanza divisions. The prose sections (vv. 1a, 19, 20-21, 22-27) return to standard paragraph format.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 15 (pages 55-56) preserves notable divergences from the MT. At v. 1, the LXX renders *ga'oh ga'ah* as *endoxōs dedoxastai* ("gloriously glorified"), shifting from "triumph/exaltation" to "glory." At v. 2, the LXX renders *ozzi vezimrat Yah* as *boēthos kai skepastēs* ("helper and protector"), reading *zimrat* as "protection" and suppressing *Yah*. At v. 3, the LXX avoids the anthropomorphic "man of war" with *suntribōn polemous* ("one who crushes wars"). At v. 8, the LXX renders *kemo-ned* ("like a heap") as *hōsei teichos* ("like a wall"), harmonizing with 14:22. At v. 13, the LXX substitutes *dikaiosunē* ("righteousness") for *chesed* ("steadfast love"). Throughout, the Vaticanus does not reproduce the *shirah* layout -- the Song is written in continuous uncial format without visual structuring.

--- SOPHIA
